attributes.stem:
A newborn boy is brought to the Emergency Department by his parents due to concerns about a rash. He was born at term via normal vaginal delivery with no complications.

On examination, the baby appears unwell. His vital signs are within normal limits for his age. He has a generalised maculopapular rash, and his head circumference is below the third percentile for his age. He is not jaundiced and has no hepatosplenomegaly. Ophthalmological examination reveals bilateral chorioretinitis. A cranial ultrasound shows intracranial calcifications and mild hydrocephalus.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely causative organism?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this newborn strongly suggests congenital toxoplasmosis, caused by Toxoplasma gondii. The combination of microcephaly, generalised rash, chorioretinitis, intracranial calcifications, and hydrocephalus is characteristic of this condition. Toxoplasma gondii is a protozoan parasite that can be transmitted from mother to fetus during pregnancy, often when the mother acquires the infection for the first time during gestation.

Cytomegalovirus (CMV) can also cause congenital infections with some similar features, including microcephaly, chorioretinitis, and intracranial calcifications. However, CMV typically causes hepatosplenomegaly and jaundice, which are not mentioned in this case. The presence of a generalised rash is more typical of toxoplasmosis.

Treponema pallidum, the causative organism of syphilis, can lead to congenital syphilis if transmitted from mother to fetus. While it can cause some similar features like rash and intracranial calcifications, it typically presents with other distinctive signs such as snuffles (rhinitis), hepatosplenomegaly, and characteristic bone changes, which are not described here.

Congenital rubella virus infection can cause microcephaly, cataracts, and hearing loss. However, the classic triad of congenital rubella syndrome includes cataracts, cardiac defects, and deafness, which are not mentioned in this case. The presence of chorioretinitis and intracranial calcifications is more suggestive of toxoplasmosis.

Zika virus can cause microcephaly and intracranial calcifications in congenital infections. However, Zika virus is not typically associated with chorioretinitis or a generalised rash in newborns. Additionally, Zika virus is geographically limited and would be less likely unless there was a history of travel to an endemic area.

The diagnosis of congenital toxoplasmosis is typically confirmed through serological testing of both mother and infant, as well as PCR testing of amniotic fluid or infant blood. Treatment usually involves a combination of pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine, along with leucovorin to prevent bone marrow suppression. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial to improve outcomes and prevent further complications.
learningPoints: Congenital toxoplasmosis can present with microcephaly, rash, chorioretinitis, intracranial calcifications, and hydrocephalus in newborns.
Toxoplasma gondii is the causative organism of congenital toxoplasmosis, transmitted from mother to fetus during pregnancy.
Pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine are the first-line treatment for congenital toxoplasmosis.
references: McAuley JB. Congenital Toxoplasmosis. J Pediatric Infect Dis Soc. 2014 Sep;3 Suppl 1(Suppl 1):S30-5. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25232475/
